### lilly antic — 2017-01-09T23:27:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-206423>

Back from Playa and the member event in Mexico.

We had a beautiful week there and I would like to share some of my experiences:

First of all a big **Thank you** to Jack and Sandy for having suggested this wonderful trip to this amazing place and resort- and then of course **THANK You** to all of you guys who were there and made this week so special!!!

Apart from great weather, sun and beach this stay was from beginning to the end filled with laughter, fun, jokes and great conversations.
I really miss you now back home and looking at the pictures brings a big grin to my face remembering those funny moments we shared together....

While there I observed some remarkable details, which make me want to explore the whole topic a bit more:

1\. Like on my stay in Cuba, I noticed a change in my sleep.
Usually I have no issues with sleeping, only a slight annoyance with the intensity and quantity of my dreams. It is great to have dreams, but if the night is filled with movie-like episodes, sometimes the morning feels exactly like that-like after a night full of movies.
In Mexico, it resembled more the dream state of my youth ( and while in Cuba)- some dreams but also some deep. deep, deep restful sleep. Like putting your head on the pillow and next thing you know it is morning.
I was wondering about that fact, as the place is filled with a high density of antennas everywhere. It must be connected to the strong magnetic field, which is found in this area....
Another remarkable fact is my weight.
We were staying in an all-inclusive resort, with many great restaurants and food around the clock.
At home I am eating mostly 1, sometimes 2 meals per day. I don't have weight issues, but was struggling the last year to get rid of some ( for my taste) annoying 2 kg, which were resistant to go, no matter what method or trick I applied.
So here I was in Playa, eating ( and complaining about the fact) all day- although there was no real hunger- the meals were more for social reasons.
You can imagine my surprise when after a few days those 2 extra kg vanished into thin air ALTHOUGH I have been eating more than usual.
(My complaining stopped of course immediately hahaha)

I am so happy that I could make it this year ( after I already missed the cruise), and am really, really looking forward to the next event.

If you can make it, I highly recommend to come. Would love to meet more of you guys and spend some time together.

So they after a few months suffering they recently found a new place, a house in the middle of nowhere, only nature around, also in Finland, and moved there, and she immediately got healthy again!

It is so crazy, but true.
So many are suffering and they have no clue....

### Sun Disciple — 2017-02-04T19:33:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-207944>

Hey All!

I am looking to visit playa del carmen in march when Jack is heading down again. Im looking to book somewhere to stay this week and the Royal and grand porto are out of my price range unfortunately this year. My question is did anyone stay anywhere else or have any suggestions for accomodations close by. I am traveling by myself as well.

### Rubicon — 2017-02-04T21:52:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-207950>

Hi Sun Disciple. I was at the 2017 event in January and stayed in a nearby apartment I found on AirBNB. The problem with doing this is that the grounds of the Royal are off limits unless you're a guest. They check everyone for armbands and it's not really practical to repeatedly sneak in without one.

About PDC:
The Royal/Gran Porto are in the touristy area of PDC and as a result the prices of everything there are substantially higher than what you might typically find in Mexico. Typically as you go from the beach into PDC the prices of everything rapidly falls. Along the beach up to 5th ave (Calle Quinta Avenida) you'll find taxis/restaurants with inflated prices. From 10th ave to 20th is a little more reasonable and you can find a taxi probably at half price. At 30th ave and up you're back in Mexico with lots of cheap places to eat and normal prices on everything...particularly if you can speak a little bit of spanish and try not to look like too much of a tourist.

Here are a few ways people did the January event, and the results they had:

Minimal expense ($30-$60 per day): stay in a hostel or a cheap AirBNB accommodation in town located 30th ave or beyond. It's practical to walk to the beach or take a taxi for 30-50 pesos each way. You can survive on cheap taco's/groceries without spending much. With this option you'll get to hang out on the beach when Jack & others are outside the hotel grounds, especially at sunrise and throughout the morning/afternoon. After sundown typically Royal guests go for dinner inside the grounds and it will be difficult/impossible to join in on that.

Medium expense ($150-$200 per day): You can buy a daypass at the Gran Royal for $105USD per day that gives you access from 9:00AM to 11:00PM without booking a room. The daypass lets you use the towels/chairs on the beach, CT tank in the spa, and eat at the restaurants (including all-you-can-eat sushi at Asiana). With this option you pretty much get the whole experience of being able to hang out with Jack & other members, but you'll have to travel back and forth to your accommodation in town.

High expense ($350-$600 per day): Stay at the Royal, all inclusive. Note: The Royal is adults only, whereas families can stay at the Gran Porto. In January, those staying at the Gran Porto could not easily access the Royal and they typically would eat apart from the main group at restaurants inside the Gran Porto.

I did the daypass thing and found it to be good value considering it includes 3 meals per day.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-02-04T23:07:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-207954>

Rubicon was the meals worth the money of the band????

### Rubicon — 2017-02-05T00:38:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-207969>

I'd say the meals were worth the cost of the band.

The restaurants I dined at on nearby 5th ave typically cost $40-$60 for dinner, and the quality wasn't spectacular. Going to 10th ave or further, I found some quality options for ~$25-$30 per plate.

At the Royal I ordered as much of anything on the menu at the Pelicanos restaurant and/or accessed the buffet for breakfast/lunch. Dinners at the seafood/sushi restaurant Asiana's featured huge platters of sashimi, roast duck, rack of lamb, etc. Good quality food all around.

### Freader — 2017-02-12T03:27:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-208439>

PDC was a wonderful experience! I'm so glad I decided to go and take my husband because he loves the sun. He is now curious and asks questions. We are both looking forward to going again in March. I'm learning to make the sun my friend and for me that is a big deal! Thanks to Sandy and Jack for their hospitality and generosity and everyone I met. I hope to see you again at another event soon!

### JMO — 2017-04-04T06:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/member-event-mexico.18802/post-211705>

We were fortunate to overlap our spring break vacation with Jack and Jeremy and a few other members. We were lucky to meet a woman at our hotel, Eva that hooked us up with an awesome taxi/tour guide. We ended up going to Tankah cenote close to Tulum in the middle of the jungle. The water was so cool and invigorating that we stayed for close to 3 hours. We tried to leave at least 3 times and ended up jumping back in...like it was calling our names. Finally Señor Cruz said he got a phone call from the owner and said we would have to leave because they were going to let the alligators out at 4...lol. We went into Tulum and had some great seafood then went to Paradise beach. The beach was beautiful but was crowded. The water beautiful blue. We walked down the beach at the edge of the water down towards the ruins then back. It was a magical day! The next day we were fortunate to hook up with Jack and Jetemy. Our 17 year old daughter was with us and it was amazing to see how Jack and Jeremy focused in on her to educate her. We have been trying to teach her about light, water and magnetism for awhile now and she has implemented some protocols but how this meeting has impacted her has blown us away! She has heard us listening to so many podcasts and our conversations about Jacks teachings but it became real when she was able to meet Jack and Jeremy and others from the forum. She has become a warrior and taken steps to help her friends. She took some Ubex glasses over to her friends and explained why she is having problems sleeping. Last weekend she was over a friends and their parents had some friends over and she was educating the adults about whtat she knew about quantum physics. The next day she dropped Uvex glasses off for one of the parents. She can't wait to go back in Dec for the next member event. The Royal is an absolutely beautiful hotel with even a cold tub and infrared sauna. I have found myself dreaming about Playa...the cenote and feeling very grateful to Jack and the others on the forum for a memorable experience for my family.

### Butters — 2016-12-01T15:11:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pulmonary-blood-pressure.18803/post-204583>

Im 26. I just had an echo echo for my heart. All functions were right, valves ok, ejection fraction 75%. The doc almost forgot to mention my pulmonary blood pressure and it was 20.5 mg/Hg. Thats on the upper end of normal. I looked up and 21-24 is slightly latent hypertension in the pulmonary artery.
So I am a bit worried.

What could be the cause and what can I do about this?
I had no asthma etc. in my past.

Edit.: The numbers are varying. I dont know if it was mean or systolic pressure. So its pretty worthless. But just for information, the question is standing there.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-01T20:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pulmonary-blood-pressure.18803/post-204594>

I would not worry...........

### Butters — 2016-12-01T22:06:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pulmonary-blood-pressure.18803/post-204598>

Ok. Thx for the quick reply. Im stressing myself over those things.

Hello All

My goal is longevity and I believe I come from a good stock of females from my mother's side. I don't want to screw up anymore this precious bloodline God has given me!, and it's time to get in the game. Thank you Dr K for giving me this opportunity for longevity. My 12 yr health quest roller coaster ride is about to end. Finally the answers I've been looking for, I'm no scientist but can get the meat off the bones, so to speak, from the webcasts, forums and blogs. I simply love the culture of this website because Dr K gives the answer at the fundamental core which absolutely resonates with my ideals of not blindly following the flock and discovering the foundational truth living by Mother Nature's laws. This reminds me of a place in western Canada called Head Smashed in Buffalo Jump - a cliff named by the Natives who chased the herd over plummeting to their death - it's kinda how our dopamine deficient society is living now. I prefer to be the hunter not the hunted. Dr K is directing us to Un-dumb down the nation starting with ourselves. He's brilliant and I love his potty mouth! Keepin' it real.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-08T04:42:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheers-from-43rd-parallel-in-sw-ontario-canada.18810/post-204965>

Tonight's factoid: Polar environments can teach those people above 50 latitude North some great things. The relevance of polar observations scales to other regions like the UK and Scandinavia where heart disease, autoimmunity, and cancer incidence grows with every decade since the 1880's. Polar regions are greater teachers of the 3 legged stool of how life is organized. Polar regions provide a useful environments for observing and optimizing the intensity and spectral composition of ambient light. Most of this light is sun light in the summer and artificial light in autumn and winter. Many circumstances in the high latitudes during winter with the short photoperiods allow for unique windows to see how humans react. When we add the complexity with an interior workplace, which are associated with a lack of bright natural light, the observations paint a clear picture of why shift work at night in low latitudes is deadly. In midwinter in the UK, for example, London at 52°N, travel to and from work will often be completed wholly in artiﬁcial light, since from 4 December to 7 January the sun rises after 08:00 h and sets before 16:00 h. Right now Londoner's are in this veyr window. Workstations may well have no windows, and outdoor activity at lunchtime or during daylight hours may be difficult, if not undesirable in poor weather conditions. Thus, data from polar regions are likely to have applicability in urban environments with winter daylengths shorter than the working day. And those observations have not been kind to those subjects. “Midwinter insomnia” has repeatedly been described for regions of northern Europe, particularly Norway, and is characterized by difficulties falling asleep. These people tend to lose Stage 4 sleep routinely. The available information suggests that internal desynchrony will be associated with metabolic abnormalities, particularly risk factors for heart disease, and possibly cancer. Similarly, cardiac function and, indeed, all physiology governed by circadian rhythms (Hastings et al., 2003) is likely to be affected. Subjects free-run infrequently in a structured polar environment in winter, but this does happen, especially after a period of night shift. Most readily adapt to the night shift, but the readaptation to day work is often very slow in winter. All of these problems can be addressed by maintaining a consolidated and suitable circadian phase. The treatment of choice, both in winter and summer, is carefully timed light exposure and avoidance as a function of endogenous phase, with or without carefully timed melatonin treatment.

### ShannyM — 2016-12-08T17:02:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheers-from-43rd-parallel-in-sw-ontario-canada.18810/post-204989>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=204969)
>
> I lived in Simcoe for many years.......... what do you grow? raise?

No Kidding!! I grew up in Scotland, ON, just up the road. My parents still live there on highway 24 in the house I grew up in....and the world continues to get smaller....

My property used to be a sheep farm when I was a kid at which time my paternal grandparents owned it. Then it was used to grow Ginseng and where I worked as a teenager. Now it's an overgrown Christmas tree farm that has about 10 acres of differing conifers which will be eventually cleared. Goats, chickens, a jersey cow and bee hives will be populating my place within the next 18 mos. Right now I do a little organic gardening, mostly dill, cilantro, oregano, sage, carrots, cherry tomatoes, strawberries, mint, cucumbers and sugar baby watermelons. Lots of mullberry trees and black caps (black raspberry) grow wild on my land.

To survive my 'non 9-5' job I try to make my schedule so I'm only flying during the day. Intuitively I haven't liked flying late at night and way prefer early mornings but the early morning report times mean I'm waking up at 3:30am 2x's per week, I presume waking up at 3:30 is better than going to bed at 3:30 am. Sleeping in hotel rooms 2xs per week on average. Yuk! I turn off as much as I can in the hotel room to limit exposure. I think blueblockers are in order for night flying to protect me. When I do work late at night and then drive home on a 1.5 + hour commute I step out of the car and my whole body is vibrating and feels like it's buzzing. What is that?!! Next time I feel like that I'll take off my shoes and socks and stand in the grass for a few minutes before entering the house. Curious if grounding will relieve it. I'm confused whether I should or shouldn't wear my serengeti sunglasses when sitting in the flight deck at 25,000 ft ASL? Is it better to have the UVA protection when airborne and and sun is bright through the windscreen?

You need to stay home on the farm and raise goats - and then make goat cheese!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-03T00:55:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-204676>

So you live in Canada home of the low UV world right? Do you have any fluoride in your life too with the shitty light? Say in your water or drugs you use? I bet you do........can this cause floaters? Yep.

Fluoroquinolones can be dangerous in people with a low redox and low levels of UV light assimilation and utilization in mitochondrial at NAD+. Why? They contain fluoride which lowers the ability of UV light to be absorbed and stored in the exclusion zone of cell water. They also cause unwinding of cellular collagen even in your lens of your eye and the vitreous. Quinolones also absorb UV light because of their quinone ring stores light inside their structure and not allowing it to make it available to the cell when fluoride is around because it is a dielectric blocker in water. There is reason to believe this affect may have long lasting effects. The possible side effects number over 100, including death, blindness, deafness, hearing loss, peripheral neuropathy, extreme muscle wasting, tendon rupture, joint pain, electric shocks in arms, legs, and chest, insomnia, anxiety, panic, brain fog, memory loss, muscle twitching, confusion, abnormal dreams, headaches, depersonalization, depression, suicidal thoughts, hallucinations, seizures, itching, hair loss, loss of collagen, wrinkles, skin rash, phototoxicity, difficulty swallowing (aphasia), abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, acid reflux, maldigestion, food sensitivities, dry eyes, **_floaters_**, double vision, retinal tears, tinnitus, dizziness, loss of balance, hearing loss, difficulty speaking, heart palpitations, tachycardia, purpura, petechiea, shortness of breath, hypotension, hypertension, lightheadedness, sweating (hyperhydrosis), lack of sweating (anhydrosis), feeling cold, unexplained weight change, hypoglycemia, diabetes, excessive thirst and urination, loss of libido, low testosterone, dental problems, extreme fatigue, chemical sensitivity, food sensitivities, and many more.
If you take Cipro, Levaquin, Avelox, or any other fluoroquinolones, including eye and ear drops, according to the FDA, you are at risk of developing any/many of these symptoms. If your water is fluoridated..........you need to do better. Become aware and talk to your doctors about these issues.

I do live in Canada. South east corner of Saskatchewan. Yes there is next to no UV light in Canada for much of the year. There is naturally occurring fluoride in the ground water that is used for the water supply in the town that I live in. They do not add any though. I am not on any meds. I do consume some supplements but only started to do so after the eye floaters started. I weigh approx 150 lbs and have never really been over weight. When I was younger I did consume large amounts of refined sugars and carbs but don't so much now. I try to follow a paleo diet but like most people I slip up now and then. I have been a chronic iPhone user for probably the past 5 years. I have spent a lot of time under artificial light and in front of a computer screen (thanks to post secondary education). I do not spend much time in front of a computer anymore and work out side quite a bit. I am starting to suffer slightly from myopia, I have been given glasses by the eye doctor but cant wear them because they make me feel messed up. It seems like some days I am myopic and others I am not. I don't know how to explain it. I also suffer from tinnitus from time to time. I do drink alcohol frequently in the form of red wine, beer or vodka and mix. I don't seem to have any trouble sleeping but my energy levels are very up and down. The floaters have caused me a great deal of stress and anxiety. I have joined many eye floater forums and facebook groups but there is no real information to help with the problem.

I read everything you posted for me 3 times over. I watched the video (way over my head).

What I am getting from all of this:
\- Eat as much DHA (sea food) as I can
\- Avoid as much artificial light as possible
\- Avoid nnEMF
\- Avoid flouride
\- Get natural UV light in the eye, which is impossible for me due to where I live. I shall look into a vacation for a week or two.

I am hopeful but I have never heard of someone's eye floater(s) disappearing, only getting worse. I will be committed and if this works I will throw my iPhone off a god damn bridge. I have also purchased your book and am hoping to learn more about this. It seems like common sense once you think about.

Jack do you know if the vitreous can be repaired? Most eye doctors say its a closed system. Once a floater forms they say its there for life.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-06T01:00:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-204840>

If you abide by your educational ignorance for a long enough time you hope you’ll stumble into wisdom, in my opinion. This small success makes you less fearful to ask the big questions about things at the boundary or circumference of our knowledge. This is how progress really leaps forward and improves the heft of our intellect. People who ask the big questions usually become innovators of new ideas. Rarely will you find them spending time worrying about their critics or skeptics in the temperate latitudes between the two spaces called good and average, where most people spend 99% of their lives. We must become more comfortable with probability and uncertainty and realizing the boundary of our knowledge and the wall of our ignorance is an easy way to do it. With nature and life it is a given that there's always the risk that we’re suffering because there are unknown unknowns. Don’t fear that……..chase them. I chase my ignorance to ask better questions all the time.

### Huck — 2016-12-06T01:53:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-204843>

Did you do anything to damage your eye?

I just had a really dumb accident a few days ago with a screw driver and hit about an inch above my right eye with the point of the screw driver. I've had 2 floaters since then. They light up at night like fireworks. I'm going to give it a few more days to see if they go away. If not, I'll probably have to see an ophthalmologist to see if there is any nerve damage.

### Dain Sorensen — 2016-12-06T17:14:16+0200

I have never had physical damage to my eyes. I am assuming any damage to my eyes is due to light. There is thousands of people like me. The type of floaters I have are often call benign. Eye doctors can not see them. They claim they are very close to the retina and that is why they are so annoying. I don't think eye doctors really know much about them. They say it is the collagen within the eye that has collapsed due to age......I acquired them when I was 27. I know people in their 70s that have never had one. I see them all day everyday in almost all lighting conditions.

I believe one facebook group I am in has approx. 32000 members. If it could be proved light was the culprit for the benign eye floaters and someone found a way to reverse them.....you would be a billionaire.

> Hello,
>
> I am new to this forum and am wondering if anyone here suffers from eye floaters? I gained these bastards approx 3 years ago and I feel my environment is the reason. Any feedback is appreciated.
>
> Thanks
> Dain

floaters:

Retinal dopaminergic amacrine (DA) cells synthesize both dopamine and GABA. Both neurotransmitters are released over the entire cell surface and act on neighboring and distant neurons by volume transmission, but, in addition, DA cells establish GABAergic synapses onto AII amacrine cells, the neurons that transfer rod signals to cone bipolars.

DA cells exert complex functions in the retina: dopamine is released upon illumination, diffuses throughout the retina and acts by volume transmission on most types of retinal neurons, setting their gain for vision in bright light. In other words, the retinal dopaminergic circuit is a visual signal amplifier. The physiological significance of the GABA released by DA cells has not been established with certainty: by inhibiting DA cells’ postsynaptic target, GABA may prevent signals of the saturated rods from entering the cone pathway when the dark-adapted retina is suddenly exposed to bright illumination. The retinal GABAergic circuit is a visual signal attenuator, and also acts a retinal melatonin activator. Dimming the light can help you increase the retinal melatonin release for sleeping or relaxation.

If a retinal dopamine neuron can not clear GABA fast enough in response to light transition, you will see an eye floater in your visual field.
If a retinal dopamine neuron can not clear GABA all the time under light, you will see an eye floater all the time when you expose your eyes to light. The eye floater size depends on how many DA cells get involved.

easy to fix

### Dimitri — 2016-12-22T02:24:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eye-floater.18814/post-205633>

You are not alone Dain, I also suffer from moderate eye floaters. They are in my right eye which interestingly enough is slightly myopic, but not to the point where I need to wear glasses. My left eye which is actually slightly beyond 20/20 does not have any floaters, however I have trouble seeing up really close with that eye.

I acquired them when I was young probably around the age of 14, right around the time my myopia also developed in the right eye. The only significant lifestyle change that happened around that time was I started spending considerably more time at the computer as I received my first computer game for Christmas that year. I may have had some biologic concrete as Dr. Kruse calls it by that time since I was not yet on the paleo diet which I did not discover until I was 19, but then again my diet was not that bad in the insulin department although maybe all the grains and pasta I was eating did more glycation damage than I realize. I was already drinking non-fluoridated water for at least a couple years by that time because we thought it was bad for other reasons as we did not know about it being a dielectric blocker. I remember eating fish about twice a week as a child and I didn't spend that much time in front of screens either. We were also some of the last people to adopt wifi which I did not have in my house until I was almost 17 probably. I also spent a lot of time outside as a kid without sunglasses on as I would play outside almost every day after school until I hit middle school.

I eventually developed adrenal fatigue when I was 19, and at the time I also got mild tinnitus in my right ear but not my left. So I know that the tinnitus was not caused by loud noises or improper use of headphones. Dr. Kruse mentioned to me that my cell phone was the likely culprit of both my tinnitus and eye floaters since they are both on my right side, and I had been using a basic flip phone for a couple of years prior to receiving the floaters although I used it very conservatively and not every day.

I have only been following Dr. Kruse's protocols for about six months now and I know these things can take time, but I will keep you posted and please let us know if you make any progress.

### Mads Haerup — 2016-12-04T14:03:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204756>

Hi, I have the recent years had Fungus on my feet and in the groin, congested nose and sore throat multiple times, hives, a coughing period lasting for several months, and mucus stuck in my throat which doesn't seem to go away. I seem to be very susceptible to getting a cold and sore throat. So I was wondering, something must be wrong with my immune system, since this happens. I have visited a few doctors, but they just prescribe medicine or tell you it will go away. I'm not a fan of that approach though. So I'm seeking some answers of what I should look for if I choose to get an immune system test done, and which hormones might play a role in this madness.
My hair has also started to thinning out, it started when I turned 18, it's in my genetics as my father is bald and my big brother has almost lost all his. If any has any inputs on how to delay or treat it, then speak your mind. (I have read jack Kruse article on this topic.)
A little about myself: 21 years, male, living in Denmark and therefore won't get exposed to as much sun which would be optimal, I'm considering investing in a humancharger to solve this problem. I rarely consume alcoholic drinks. I only eat organic food, mostly plant based due to the high prices of organic meat, I eat meat 1-3 times a week though. I don't consume junk food, sugary food/beverages, eating as little gluten as possible. I often fast for 12-14 hours, So I skip breakfast every morning. I have an active job as a demolisher, I'm walking around 10 km/6 miles a day, and then I do strength based workout 3-4 times a week. I workout on an empty stomach 2 times a week. I have no allergies, dairy tolerant, gluten tolerant. I drink tons of herbal tea a day, consume broccoli, spinach, sweet potato, blueberries, avocado almost every day. So I should get an adequate amount of antioxidants. Supplements I take: Organic Spirulina powder, Ginger capsules, MSM, Green lipped mussels, Fish oil either Artic COD liver oil or an organic danish fish oil. I try to limit my exposure to harmful chemicals, I only use organic personal hygiene products and organic/sustainable soap for dishes, clothes, and for cleaning my home.

Last info: I Broke my foot 4 months ago and have been exposed to CT-scan 2 times this year, and as I understand CT emits a fairly amount of radiation which also could play a role in altering my cells and immune system.
Hope some of you can help me out a bit
Cheers!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:27:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204767>

When you are missing Selenium semiconductors in your tissues diseases like mold/candida/fungus/cancer are much more likely because of a loss of immunoregulation. Selenium is needed for the proper functioning of the immune system cells because of how it moves electrons and works with sunlight. It is a co-factor in the activation of neutrophils, macrophages, and Natural killer cells and T lymphocytes and many other immunoregulatory functions as laid out in Ferencik’s paper in 2003. Selenium seems to act via solid state mechanisms by two modes to protect us from cancer. One is that is catalyzes a many immune reactions to increase our immune function in the GALT. Secondly, Se seems to hinder oncogenesis by protecting the p53 gene by adding electrons to this gene in the gut mitochondria and does not allow oncogenesis to occur. In fact, it appears to favor low level inflammation by altering NRF2 pathways. This is seen in infectious diseases and is seen in autoimmune conditions vs. cancer, and thus, favors apoptosis of defective cells instead of oncogenesis.

It appears that low plasma Selenium levels reduce the production of plasma glutathione peroxidase. Patients with low selenium levels are known to have higher rates of polyps and autoimmunity. (Fernandez-Banares et al., 2002) Naturally occurring Selenium is found in plants like garlic and broccoli as a compound called Se-methylselenocysteine (Se-MSC). Seafood is the best source!!! Se-MSc produces 33% reduction in cancerous lesions (colon) than selenite (synthetic). It turns out cysteine is really important too with Selenium. It also produces a 50% decrease in new tumor development locally in the gut. It also induces cells to undergo apoptosis and not epi-oncogenesis. It has been shown to reduce angiogenesis to make the cellular terroir less hospitable to cancer. Se- MSC also directly down regulates vascular endothelial growth factor that is commonly found in most GI cancers. So the story seems to show that selenium is pretty critical to gut health and disease prevention because it is an N-type semiconductor.

In fact, many people believe that cystic fibrosis is a severe form of selenium deficiency in the germ cell line and not a real genetic disease. This is controversial today, but I believe it has legs. It has also been seen in numerous papers that in both autoimmune and oncogenesis disease that sulfated vitamin D3 and Selenium decreases are seen much more commonly linked. Vitamin K2 production by the gut bacteria is also down regulated in these cases as well in inflammatory conditions like cancer, autoimmunity and inflammatory bowel diseases. It appears that chronic gut inflammation allows for the reduction of both selenium, sulfated vitamin D3 and vitamin K2. All of these molcules are photoactive with sunlight because of their carbon cages in the aromatic amino acids and quinone rings. These rings act like a play ground for photons to build up the DC electric current in cells used to activate the immune system. Vitamin K2 reduction also shows a very strong correlation with many neolithic diseases as well tied to poor solar exposure.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204768>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27217104](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27217104)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:30:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204770>

Every single study you find links dietary marine intake correlating with decreases in inflammation. Why?
These foods have things like B12 and Se in them, therefore they collects photons and move electrons in these tissues is the short answer. Where ever you problem is with inflammation realize you need DHA and the co-factors buried in the marine seafood chain.

Consider the cell mediated immune system. Its most critical cell that activates both arms of immunity need the macrophage to work.

We've known for a long time that DHA tames inflammation, now, we learn exactly how DHA works: via new substances called maresins. These chemicals are breakdown chaperone proteins from DHA that change Macrophages from inflammatory to anti inflammatory actors in the immune system. This means that macrophages change their function with the addition or subtraction of electrons in maresins.
DHA does this by switching them from M1 to M2 phenotypes in this immune cell line. This cell is very commonly found in the human GI tract. If you got a gut problem........you need the Epi paleo Rx.

You need oysters. If you do not like them........then enjoy your illness.

This is a critical spot in autoimmune activation. We encounter inflammation almost daily in our species, but our body has ways of turning it off naturally. DHA is that switch in CMI in macrophages. This is an important step toward understanding exactly this happens. You're likely to be hearing a lot more about maresins when medicine realizes that DHA is an electron story.

In the July 2013 issue of The FASEB Journal, researchers found that macrophages use DHA to produce the chaperone like "maresins," which serve as the "switch" that turns inflammation off and switches on resolution in cell mediated reactions.

CITES:
Jesmond Dalli, Min Zhu, Nikita A. Vlasenko, Bin Deng, Jesper Z. Haeggström, Nicos A. Petasis, and Charles N. Serhan. The novel 13S,14S-epoxy-maresin is converted by human macrophages to maresin 1 (MaR1), inhibits leukotriene A4 hydrolase (LTA4H), and shifts macrophage phenotype. FASEB J July 2013 27:2573-2583; doi:10.1096/fj.13-227728

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-04T20:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immune-system.18818/post-204772>

Selenium deficiency is associated with many cancer lines as well. Selenium deficiency has been shown to cause global hypomethylation in DNA. This links it to B-12, another solar vitamin. This is where the link of infectious disease, autoimmunity and oncogenesis seems to lie. Hypo-methylation is the “on and off switch” between the these two disease processes. Hypo-methylation is more apparent when DNA is not well hydrated in vivo. The co factors important for switch activation are selenium, sulfated Vitamin D3, and bacterial Vitamin K2 production in the leaky gut. The differing concentrations of all three seem to predispose to what cellular fate or field will be faced in presence of a leaky gut. The less aggressive cellular terroir may predispose us GI distress with bloating, malnutrition and bad bowel habits. It may progress to an elevated sdLDL cholesterol that causes chronic neolithic diseases like heart disease, Crohn’s disease or diabetes. More significant inflammation likely elevates IL-1b and favors autoimmune conditions to blossom. This is associated with NF kappa beta and IL-6 spikes in the brain by way of the vagus nerve. This is when the blood brain barrier becomes leaky. We can assess this with a "GABA test" clinically. Further inflammation changes the mix of the co factors and the cytokines to favor frank oncogenesis in many of our gut organs. We have no idea what is critical to each step but we do know the players today. So if you alter your solar exposure and diet toward seafood make sure each one is plentiful you can control the master switch in your own “leaky gut” to control your outcome. When it comes to the leaky blood brain barrier, the key issue seems to be an environmental triggers of nnEMF and blue light that alters T helper cell maturation in the neuro-immune system. N-type semiconductors have a larger electron concentration than hole concentration. The phrase ‘n-type’ comes from the negative charge of the electron. In n-type semiconductors, electrons are the majority carriers and holes are the minority carriers. N-type semiconductors are created by doping an intrinsic semiconductor with donor impurities (or doping a p-type semiconductor as done in the making of CMOS chips). A common dopant for n-type silicon in technology is phosphorus. Life does the same thing, but it pairs carbon with nitrogen and phosphorus in its semiconductive circuits. Selenium works with Ca2+ and Mg2+. Physics geeks will recall in an N-type semiconductor, the Fermi level is greater than that of the intrinsic semiconductor and lies closer to the conduction band than the valence band. You might want to read the Time 21 blog.

# Thread: The place you choose to get an MRI and those who read it have implications
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-place-you-choose-to-get-an-mri-and-those-who-read-it-have-implications.18819/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-05T04:14:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-place-you-choose-to-get-an-mri-and-those-who-read-it-have-implications.18819/post-204787>

If you have not listened to the podcast Idid with Ben Greenfield on his MRI and what I found on it you might find it interesting. Have a listen to it over at his site. Most radiologists are not trained to see images of the redox potential so what you get is very uneven results. The redox potential of the people reading your scan often matters more than the redox potential in the scan!!!! Most people never get to chose where to have their scan done so they have no idea what variables matter. Moreover, they never meet the radiologist to get a sense of his or her own redox potential which is a critical vriable too. Most radiologists spend their shift in a dark room surrounded by blue light monitors for 8-10 hours a days. Usually experience grows as one ages in medical fields. I think in radiology we might have question that axiom because of what they are doing to their eye clock. In the last 15 years I have not met one radiologist who is not mypoic of who does not wear glasses of contacts yet no one ever thinks about what this means to the frontal lobes attached to those eyes. In radiology, this might mean that the longer they do this job, the worse they maybe at interpreting scans. This is why I always look at who gave me a reading of a scan and then I really dissect the report with the image myself. This lets me observe if the reading is coherent to the image or not. This is a trick I learned long ago from one of my mentors. It is a bigger deal as time has elapsed because of how neuroimaging has changed. Now we have this study belwo that shows you this phenomena has some real clinical consequences that rarely get spoken about. You can find the podcast I did with Ben here. [https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/2016/11/measuring-redox-potential-with-mri/](https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/2016/11/measuring-redox-potential-with-mri/)

MRI's are basically quantum super position images of proton shadows. They are subject to machine/image bias and observer bias because both machine and eye reading it are quantum machines that work on probability and uncertainty principles. The real issue.........no one realizes it. Well, some of us do and I hope you consider the implications of this post.

STUDY: Patient gets 10 MRIs done at different facilities on her lumbar spine in a 3 week period and the reports are all over the place.

Where you go and who reads the scans can be the difference between you getting the right diagnosis and the wrong one. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/27867079/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/27867079/)

### Richelle Jones — 2017-04-23T08:47:14+0300

Is having an MRI bad for me, having HD? I used to participate in the HOPES study for HD and underwent an MRI every two years for about 6 years. I always came out of the machine with massive brain fog.

In the last 15 years I have not met one radiologist who is not mypoic of who does not wear glasses of contacts yet no one ever thinks about what this means to the frontal lobes attached to those eyes. In radiology, this might mean that the longer they do this job, the worse they maybe at interpreting scans. This is why I always look at who gave me a reading of a scan and then I really dissect the report with the image myself. This lets me observe if the reading is coherent to the image or not. This is a trick I learned long ago from one of my mentors. It is a bigger deal as time has elapsed because of how neuroimaging has changed. Now we have this study belwo that shows you this phenomena has some real clinical consequences that rarely get spoken about. You can find the podcast I did with Ben here. [https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/2016/11/measuring-redox-potential-with-mri/](https://bengreenfieldfitness.com/2016/11/measuring-redox-potential-with-mri/)
>
> MRI's are basically quantum super position images of proton shadows. They are subject to machine/image bias and observer bias because both machine and eye reading it are quantum machines that work on probability and uncertainty principles. The real issue.........no one realizes it. Well, some of us do and I hope you consider the implications of this post.
>
> STUDY: Patient gets 10 MRIs done at different facilities on her lumbar spine in a 3 week period and the reports are all over the place.
>
> Where you go and who reads the scans can be the difference between you getting the right diagnosis and the wrong one. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/27867079/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/27867079/)

Great podcast with Ben, you surprised him (and me) with your diagnosis of his MRI. A case in point; I had an MRI done of my knee and received a diagnosis of what and where the injury was. When I had surgery to "repair" the injury, what was found was not what was "seen" on the MRI. Since then I have been a bit skeptical of MRIs.

### JoeT — 2016-12-06T04:19:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nj.18824/post-204846>

Hi All, new member here, first posting!

This seems like a great community, and I am looking forward to participating. I'd like to thank all of you, especially Mr. Kruse, for all of the knowledge sharing!

A little about me: I luckily don't have any pressing health issues...I'm always trying to improve the way I feel, and my performance. I'm not even sure how I stumbled on to Jack's work; it may have been either the Greenfield or Ausprey podcasts.

Unfortunately, I don't really have a passion for reading Science; I wish I did, but I don't. That being said, I find Jack's work reading the superior importance of light, grounding, and water to be absolutely brilliant.
So simple, yet so overlooked. Everything we need is right here in front of us, if we can just ditch our modern ways a little bit. So, while I have only skimmed many of the blogs and Q&A's, I am excited to see how my body reacts to application of the concepts.

Due to family comitments, I can't leave NJ, so a lot of my work thus far has been in measuring, eliminating, and reducing EMFs, and increasing Light. In my first two weeks, I've implemented the following, and am planning a lot more:

1\. 10-15 minutes outside at sunrise; no glasses and grounded.
2\. purchased a trifield and made numerous little adjustments at work and at home, including moving the room I sleep in, and position of computers.
3\. Uvex after dark.
4\. More sun at 11 am and 1 pm
5\. Added sardines and shrimp weekly
6\. Ordered Thera Blue glasses for Office
7\. Phone is now almost always on airplane
8\. Shut off Wifi at night.
9\. I've always drank at least 72 oz of RO per day, so no change there.

Things I'm working On
1\. Getting Baseline lab work....my last Bun to Creatine was 13. My cholesterol has always been sensitive to Saturated Fat.
2\. Getting a better meter for RF; getting Ethernet back.
3\. Potentially starting to drive to work instead of taking train....pending.
4\. Formally following one or two JK protocols, probably CT.
5\. Just got Jack's book.
6\. If i start to see real results from the above, I will almost certainly buy a magnetico.

### JanSz — 2016-12-08T15:19:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nj.18824/post-204979>

I'm new to Epi-Paleo, and am trying to adjust my schedule, routine, and habits to get as much sun as possible given my office job. I'm up to 15 minutes at 730 am, and 10 minutes at 11 am., and 10 minutes at 1 pm. Just wondering, based on the below, if I should add some lighting in my office to offset the standard florescent? Also, if I picked up the UVB Desert light, I'm guessing I would want to start with just a minute or two every few days, correct?
\==============================================
UVB Desert light
is non descriptive
but I am guessing that you mean:
**Exo Terra Solar-Glo High Intensity Self-Ballasted Uv/Heat Mercury Vapor Lamp, 160-Watt by Exo Terra**
[https://www.amazon.com/Exo-Terra-So...qid=1481202048&sr=8-7&keywords=Exo+Terra+160w](https://www.amazon.com/Exo-Terra-Solar-Glo-Intensity-Self-Ballasted/dp/B01M4NCYAY/ref=sr_1_7?ie=UTF8&qid=1481202048&sr=8-7&keywords=Exo+Terra+160w)

This are good full spectrum lamps.
They are meant to provide full daylight needs for a lizard.
Note, lizard will be real close to such a lamp.
Light power decreases wit a square root of distance.
So, if lizard is ok having one lamp at the distance of 2 feet
At the distance of 4 feet he will need four of such a lamps to get same amount of light.
Practically, have maximum practical amount of this lamps in place where you spend most of the time,
and be aware that you will still be missing on light
if your ideal light
is light in tropics.

Speaking of light, we need full spectrum,
starting with UVb, UVa, ... blue, ... red, IR-A, heat
@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) discussions, and references (dr Wunch)
high some specific frequencies
UVb--vitamin B
UVa--vitamin A
blue (460-480nm), pineal gland, melatonin/cortisol/Circadian rhythm, on at wakeup, off at least 2-4hrs before bed time
Red, health, natural night vision
IR-A, health, night vision with instruments
heat, free energy that we live off
\------

In Woodbridge NJ you will always be short of natural UVa & UVb
I got for my self Red Sperti and use it 15min/day, average 5-6 days/week
[https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001ID8P78/ref=sr\_ph\_1\_a\_it?ie=UTF8&qid=1481203063&sr=sr-1&keywords=sperti](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001ID8P78/ref=sr_ph_1_a_it?ie=UTF8&qid=1481203063&sr=sr-1&keywords=sperti)

**Sperti Fiji SUN Table Top Tanning Lamp**
That lamp is red in color, and cost less than white lamp.

Question: I have managed to get my Office down to .5 to .8 milligause, and my RF also is reading very low. This is surprising I think since I'm basically sitting on the NJ side of the Hudson, staring out at the WTC and Empire State buildings!

So, one way I got the MG down was to shut off a florescent light that hovers about 2 feet over my desk (not a ceiling light). That was putting out around 2 Mg at 2 feet away.

So I'm wondering, would adding a light like the one below to get some increased UV (in addition to wearing my blue block glasses), have benefits that would outweigh the increased Mg that it would likely expose me to ? I recognize natural light is always best, its just very very difficult for me to get out there during peak times.

We are talking office.
I assume that you are in that office at the time when outside is a daylight.
Natural daylight contains blue light.
I think that one do not need blue-blockers during daytime (anytime).
If I follow Jack's recommendations correctly,
there is one lady here, that have eye disease and works in windowless building with fluorescent lights only.
She was a candidate for blue-blockers.
Possibly few others.
Blue-blockers have a logical application after sundown, to protect (mostly) pineal gland or other areas that may be sensitive to blue.
\--
About UV frequencies.
We need them to produce vit A (UVa) and vit D (UVb)
with help of
carotenoids (carrots, peppers)
and
sulfur (garlic, onions, MSM)
Using UV lamps during the day may be nice conversation piece.
But, you may also discover some proteins left by wandering rodents.
Those lights are also called actinic.
As far as I can tell, UV light does not affect our Circadian Rhythm.
We can have exposure to UV any time.
I feel more secure of getting sufficient UV exposure by
not taking vit D supplements
I still take vit A (but not sure)
use most sunlight within plus minus 2 hrs of solar noon
use Red Sperti. I use 15min/day about 5-6x/week

Trust but verify.
I use Spectracell Micronutient Analysis to figure (functional) level of 35 of them, including A&D
\-------------------------------

Solar noon.
In summer, in NJ, we have summer daylight saving time, clock is pushed 1hr ahead.
in summer the window for best available UV light is 11AM-3PM
in winter we are back to 10AM-2PM
\------------------------------
@[JoeT](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19594/) let me know if you are familiar with ICD-10

### JoeT — 2016-12-17T17:39:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nj.18824/post-205398>

Jan, I've been reading, researching, and listening to Q&A podcasts to try to enhance my light conditions, and have come up with the following plan, based on what is feasible given my current schedule. Can you tell me if it appears reasonable and balanced?

\- Firstly, given my office job, I'm exposed to a lot of nnEMF blue light. I am maximizing every available minute to get natural light. I watch the sun rise for 15 minutes each morning, and go outside for lunch for one hour at noon. So, i unfortunately I think I need some artificial light.

\-I don't think the Solar Glo's will work for me because 1) I'm not even sure my employer would allow it, and 2) I am still exposed to significant artificial blue light at work, so if I understand Jack correctly, I think it would be more beneficial to block all of the nnEMF blue light at work. This will require me to wear BlueTechs, which is fine.

\-During a podcast, Jack mentioned that if he doesn't get adaqate sun during the day, he will "catch up" from 4-7 with the following:
black CFL 26W for UVA, along with a Ruby Lux for NIR. Do you think I should use more than one black CFL against the Ruby lux?

\- For UVB, I have two options. I can buy a Sperti; or, it so happens that a family member, due to a skin condition, has a "Panosol II" machine in the house. I'm hesitant to try it though, as it is quite powerful. Don't even know where to start with that one.

I know this is not nearly optimal, but it's what I have to work with right now.

About UV light (UVa & UVb)
we look at it at least two ways.

High intensity, producing vit A & D, less exposure time may be need, adequacy measured by blood testing.

Low intensity, requires many hours daily, measured by overall health, non-specific
See restaurant crew in Chicago's restaurant.
Also current Scompy's posting:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/scompys-journal.16641/page-37#post-205360](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/scompys-journal.16641/page-37#post-205360)
and Jack's comments about it.

UV light presence or absence does not influence Circadian Rhythm.
Dr @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) uses one black light in his daughter bedroom.
But there is a blog written by his guest, and he mentions about 40 of those in Jack's residence.
I try to replicate that situation with Exo Terra Solar Glow 160W being on about +12hrs/day.
\----------------------------------
Blue light
is observed by pineal gland to know about time of the day.
So wearing blu-blockers when (outside) sun is up is usually wrong move.
\----------------------------------
Sunlight have a range of frequencies.
Visible is its mid-range
On each side we have frequencies that are invisible to our eyes.
We need them all, we evolved using all of them.
UV as written above.
On the other side there are variety of IR frequencies then heat.

Lots of discussions on EZ water assumes availability of IR.
That is free energy that life uses to continue.

Would it counter the Leptin RX at all if low intensity exercise was performed? I'm thinking of an intensity of a 1-3 on a 10 scale. Exercise like short walks & mobility/physical therapy work.

Thanks

### ShannyM — 2016-12-06T20:05:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannyms-journal.18826/post-204864>

Grandmother:

Born in The Netherlands 1911 #7 of 13 kids, her mother died from infection from miscarriage at 49!!
country folk, farmer, her father made wooden shoes
moved to Canada after the war 1950
birthed 6 children no miscarriages
gall stones removed at 50
in 80s developed congestive heart failure
menopause at 55!
always heavy around the middle (barrel shaped)
very strong and healthy woman
hated doctors lol
in 80s developed ulcer on esophagus
89 fell out of bed in nursing home broke hip
infection from hip led to death (?) in 90th year

during gallstone operation doctors couldnt get her under anesthetic and she ended up with too much for too long and then couldnt wake her up after which she literally went crazy and put in psych ward for 2 to 4 weeks, did some kind of shock therapy to rewire her brain, (so the story goes, anyway) which I guess worked and she was good for the rest of her life.
​

​

Mother:

Born in The Netherlands 1949 (May baby)
moved to Canada 1950 at 9 months old
always lived in the country
youngest of 6
3 children all breast fed, no miscarriages
extreme nausea during pregnancies basically from conception to delivery
PMS nausea, cramping and slightly moody normal cycles
started menopause at 45 ended at 50
never larger than size 10 now a 6
susceptible to bouts of mild depression - never diagnosed
never used oral contraceptives
hairdresser and homemaker
never been hospitalized and never a broken bone
​

Me:

47 yo (July baby)
mild eczema as child on legs until 18 yo
oral contraceptives from 17 to 29
copper IUD from 35 to 39 which my body rejected on its own
2 children now 16 and 12 - oldest son born c-section, daughter born VBAC - 100% natural
breast fed both my kids until 4.5 months
generally constipated 2 days per week when at work because of early morning starts
32-35 day cycle
airline captain turboprop so all flying done below 25,000 ft
sleep in hotels 2 nights per week
I fly 15 days a month average 10 hour days plus 2 hour commute
Im sure exposed to stupid amounts of nnEMF, too scared to find out, all glass cockpit
consistently 30 pounds overweight mostly on my tummy otherwise weight distribution is proportional
have done fasts and cleanses 2xs per year for 12 years
doing Leptin reset 8 weeks now but gained 6 - 8 pounds
adopted Mitochondria Rx protocol noticed a change in energy
early to bed kinda girl 9:30
healthy libido
12 years ago suffered from adrenal fatigue after the birth of daughter, cortisol levels out of whack couldnt sleep past 2am to 6am
single mom now for past 5 years, married for 10 stressful years
all amalgams removes 10 years ago
regular chiropractic care for 8 years
calcium build up, bone spur at c7 from minor injury at 25
huge lack of energy takes me 2 days to recover from flying for 3 days straight
my sleep has been pretty good
I love alcohol but have cut it out, love coffee and I still drink 3 cups per day
wore sunglasses everyday, brainwashed to think I had to wear them, havent worn them for 6 weeks
just started wearing 1.25 reading glasses always had 20/13 eyesight
​

The big picture tells me my epigenetics predisposes me to estrogen dominance and/or low progesteroneI need to improve my circulation and look after my heart
The nnEMF, enviromental toxins, pressures from work including my commute, are messing me up big time!!

When I started Leptin reset I actually think I went through a detox sickness. I was doing quick polar bear dips in the pool 5 nights a week after my far infrared sauna, then hot tub then another dip in the pool before bed. It helped my sleep but it felt like my body was pissed off at me for the change in BAB combined with CT so I slacked off the sauna and pool dip.
I want to have the energy I did when I was younger, lose 30 pounds, fix my hormones, and live to be over 100.

Next full on CT and continue epi-paleo diet.

I take krill oil, 3 types magnesium (malate, orotate, theonate) co-enzyme Q10 especially when flying, lots and lots of ice water.

​

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-06T20:30:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannyms-journal.18826/post-204867>

Welcome. I'll post some random ideas.

So how does sunlight help blood vessels? Well many ways as I have discussed here before but one way you might not know is via the sex steroid hormone pathways of progesterone. In males, luteinizing hormone (LH) causes the production of testosterone, whereas in females it affects estrogen and progesterone levels.....so why are you E2 dominat and devoid of progesterone and dehydrated? ............ In females, luteinizing hormone works on the ovaries--where it makes its effects by changing the levels of estradiol. The levels made are tied to the amount or lack of sun one gets. The more blue light one gets the more estrogen on makes and the less progesterone one makes. Sunlight increases both sulfated Vitamin D3 and progesterone in case you did not know this. People should be more aware that progesterone has many important functions: it looks after the smoothness of blood vessels; The link below shows you how these pathways affect are arterial health. Progesterone also affects the myelin sheaths of nerves in men, women and children and it links why MS is tied to a lack of solar exposure; and it prevents water retention in the brain of men, women and children and this affects the amount of CSF that is made and recycled in the brain.

Progesterone receptors are found, among other sites, in the endothelial lining of blood vessels. It’s those receptors that keep endothelial lining nice and smooth. With excess estrogen, the exact opposite happens: the endothelium becomes lumpy and bumpy, therefore excessive estrogen (unbalanced by progesterone) makes us prone to clotting diseases, strokes and angina. Also, the ability of the blood vessels to relax is related to the presence of progesterone receptors and sufficient progesterone level to stimulate them. Insufficient progesterone may affect one way or another the many systems of the body, including blood vessels. Men who do not have sufficient amount of progesterone tend to get cardiovascular diseases in their 40s, whereas women are protected until after their 50s. In other words, progesterone is necessary to maintain cardiovascular health.
Progesterone facilitates healing process after an injury. If you look at my pre operative considerations blog see what I say there about progesterone. Trauma, including that resulting from cerebral accidents, heals quicker in pre-menopausal women than in men because they have more progesterone. Also, they have far less water retention in the brain. For that reason some neurosurgeons (ME) use progesterone injections prior to surgery to prevent swelling due to water retention.

It is made in the brain and stimulates BDNF with appropriate levels of cortisol to get the proper physiologic effects. In women, it is made the corpus luteum of the ovary and in smaller amounts by the adrenal glands. In men, is produced in adrenal and testicular tissue as well. Progesterone is produced in the body from cholesterol and is a precursor to most of the other steroid hormones, including cortisol and testosterone. Look at Hormone 101 blog post or BG 12 as well to explain why prolonged stress further depletes progesterone because it increases the demand for cortisol. I think doctors should consider progesterone supplementation for men in their late forties and older, when they experience low energy or fatigue, decreased libido, have increased body fat, enlarged prostate or to want to help prevent prostate enlargement.Progesterone is needed to counter-balance the effects of excess estrogens. Also, by blocking the enzyme 5a-reductase, progesterone inhibits the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which is also a stimulant of prostate cell growth. This is why sunlight stimulates LH and testosterone as we talked about on this page yesterday.Benefits for men:-

Protects against prostate cancer

\- Helps normalize blood sugar levels

\- Helps use fat for energy

\- Prevents water accumulation (acts as mild diuretic)

\- Helps (normalizes) thyroid hormone function

\- Stimulates new bone formation (osteoporosis protection and even reversal)

\- Improves brain function, has antidepressant properties

\- Improves skin problems including acne, seborrhea, rosacea, psoriasis

\- Diminishes muscular aches and pains, has anti-inflammatory properties

\- Improves sleep pattern

\- Improves libido.

In women and men is also makes hair growth big time. When I hear a lady is losing her hair I know she is deficient in sunlight and thyroid hormone and progesterone due to her blue light toxic world. We see this after craniotomy when we use it after we open a head on the incision line.
[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/26469335](https://www.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F26469335&h=GAQF1Znp8AQFTaF43kqm94Cn1x3LBM00pB5WTJzWS3PZZTQ&enc=AZMrpOkO6caymV8NiNYzTdCmF_p944SRMKrlO5Uxo1GxkN_i5GF34nMDcWSJciNIa02Pt2tEr0UDFLGtwJdZrkTf4JZ5p2Hpn1EmvEIP_XOM9OUBti8KGI2NJ8A05s8Kgg9AcJ1FpfKRKBLfKsc-5qOKcb6pzFyTXJ5d_xC3YJaZK0MLVq-2MdH9ct97sZZ9UbXiaQvtINlnjYYy8y_NGVqn&s=1)

The bottom left shows you what a chloroplast, the stolen bacteria of plants does.......it consumes water to make sugar for energy. Your stolen bacteria, called a mitochondria, takes any carbon source like fat/glucose and burns it using oxygen and to make water for your cells. What is water? It is a battery (EZ) for sunlight to use for energy. What you described above is a life lived in a low energy state. There is a lot of data in this response. Study it. **Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannyms-journal.18826/post-205081>

To survive my 'non 9-5' job I try to make my schedule so I'm only flying during the day. Intuitively I haven't liked flying late at night and way prefer early mornings but the early morning report times mean I'm waking up at 3:30am 2x's per week, I presume waking up at 3:30 is better than going to bed at 3:30 am. Sleeping in hotel rooms 2xs per week on average. Yuk! I turn off as much as I can in the hotel room to limit exposure. I think blueblockers are in order for night flying to protect me. When I do work late at night and then drive home on a 1.5 + hour commute I step out of the car and my whole body is vibrating and feels like it's buzzing. What is that?!! Next time I feel like that I'll take off my shoes and socks and stand in the grass for a few minutes before entering the house. Curious if grounding will relieve it. I'm confused whether I should or shouldn't wear my serengeti sunglasses when sitting in the flight deck at 25,000 ft ASL? Is it better to have the UVA protection when airborne and and sun is bright through the windscreen?

I would quiet the buzzing with alcohol...bad bad bad! I don't do that anymore.

Epi Paleo Rx: pros so far: smoother younger skin, tighter pores, good sleep, no cravings, better mood, less PMS, sinus issues improving - less inflammation in ears and eustachian

tubes and lymph nodes in neck - chronic for years.​

cons so far: 8 pound weight gain???!!!...I didn't sign up for that! , little acne, some eczema, nausea, headaches

Mitochondria Rx: way way more energy, can stay awake at night longer, actually need less sleep. less aches and pains in neck and back, less constipation​

### ShannyM — 2016-12-22T18:38:34+0200

BAB: Salmon steak, 3 eggs, 5 slices of bacon and 1 tbsp of butter.

Yesterday I drank at least 3 litres of RO as close to freezing as possible.

Slept 10 hours last night!....cat woke me up at 5am so I stoked the wood stove and peed on a keto stick. How deep do I have to get into ketosis according to the colour on the stick? so far the reading is always a trace @ 0.5mmo/L and always negative for glucose.

Does it matter? I don't know what else I could do to get deeper into ketosis if need be.

Very watery stools without the associated diarrhea cramping. Should I cut back on the Mg? I take close to 2000mg of 4 different types of magnesium. I sense my body is purging. My thirst has waned somewhat. I wake up soo thirsty all the time.

I'm coming up to ovulation soon, curious how my cycle and PMS are this month. After eating a BAB I'm just not hungry for the rest of the day, could care less about eating supper, I think that has to do with ovulation on the horizon. I'll just go with it, if I'm not hungry later in the day I won't force myself to eat.

Got a slight headache again this morning, winded and I don't feel refreshed for the amount of sleep I got last night.

Body comp is changing even though the scale hasn't moved much. **Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-foods-from-different-regions-in-the-world.18827/post-204873>

Hey, I've been following the forums for while but this is my first post. I have a question that's been bugging me for a while but haven't been able to find much on advice on...

So from what I understand, it's best to eat food sourced and produced locally. So the light information encoded in the food matches your body's mode (that is also affected by location and environment). I'm hacking that statement to pieces, but my question is - I'm on a ketogenic diet (deeper now in winter) and I'll often eat coconut oil, almonds, tinned sardines, dark chocolate (foods that are definitely \*not\* grown and produced around London), is this bad practice? I mean, I enjoy eating the foods too - is there is a way to offset any potential effects from it?

# Thread: 34 yr old composer, cancer survivor with heart disease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/34-yr-old-composer-cancer-survivor-with-heart-disease.18830/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 51
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Danny Cocke — 2016-12-07T02:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/34-yr-old-composer-cancer-survivor-with-heart-disease.18830/post-204889>

I'm a 34 yr old composer for film and games, living in Los Angeles (EMF Hell hole! haha). I was diagnosed with testicular cancer 12 years ago, and recently was diagnosed with heart disease due to my familial hyperlipidemia.

I had a CT scan for checking for blockage in the arteries recently and to my dismay, found I had 23% blockage. The funny thing is, you would never guess it by looking at me. I look as fit and healthy as can be. I have a steady practice of Kundalini/Hatha yoga, running, walking, hiking, biking, trampolene-ing, meditating, strength training, etc.

I've been spending the last years of my cancer remission learning everything I can about health and experimenting with supplements, diets, detoxes, and on and on. In hearing the news of heart disease, LDL at 360, HDL 54, low testosterone, infertility, and a few other issues, I enlisted LA's best doctor's and endocrinologist to help me with a path away from worsening this.

The main problem for me with learning about all of this paradigm shifting thinking of Jack's work, is the disparaging recommendations. Doctor, nutritionalists, etc all recommending Ornish's book, a vegan diet with absolutely NO saturated fats, claiming coconut oil in particular as the devil (with lots of published material siting it as an artery clogging culprit, even worse than butter). I feel a lot of hesitation arising from the idea of taking ghee or coconut oil fats or eating red meat. Though fish I'm fine with, avocados, nuts, hemp, kelps, etc. I'm taking blue algae and DHA algae supplements as well (not compared to real sea food I know). I do wonder if this is an issue where I have to have a limited or restricted Epi-Paleo diet. Avoiding the saturated fats in those forms that lay on plaque in layers (supposedly).

Problem is, going with this vegan diet, high in vegetables, grains like oats, quinoa, brown rice, etc I've actually felt like my testosterone has gone down further, and my mental focus, memory and other capacities feel a bit diminished. My energy is great otherwise, just mentally and libido I really feel it. Dopamine as well, and over all creativity, inspiration feels diminished. Cannabis seems to help this in small doses.

I just read Jack's Epi-Paleo rx book front to back, and I've been listening to about 100 hours of his podcasts and presentations in the last couple weeks. It's blown my mind and really resonated with me on a deep level. I've begun virtually all of the protocols this week, diet, routine, sunlight, cold showers, eating fish & kelp, no carbs, minimizing blue light with glasses, and I even pulled all the LED lights from my studio and switched to incandescent bulbs. My work days in my EMF microwave cave of a studio I'm going shorter with more focused intention for the day. I'm doing my yoga in the sun, and morning meditation in the sunrise in my underwear outside every morning.

So I just had labs done this week and will see what my baseline is soon. I Then hope to follow this protocol intensely and re-check in 90 days to see if I've had progress, before any taking any statins, or testosterone therapy, which the doctors all recommend and I vehemently deny. Only hang up I have is the high saturated fats, ghee, coconut oil, red meat, etc. But otherwise, very thrilled to leap in.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-07T03:06:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/34-yr-old-composer-cancer-survivor-with-heart-disease.18830/post-204890>

Welcome. Do not be afraid of fats. But you have to realize your heterplasmy rate in mitochondria is the key to your game plan.

What have I learned so far about my own mitochondria from ten years of bio-hacks? Why are summer time high fat diets not a great thing to do chronically across all seasons? High fat diets force the use of mitochondria because they are oxidative powerhouses for fat. But what if you are designed to be loosely coupled by your mtDNA haplotype? Food guru's and journalists never consider this. If you have a Northern Europe haplotype, it means you are loosely coupled. Loosely coupled means that there is quantized deficit between how the mitochondria and nucleus in a cell work to work at higher latitudes where solar light is not present to excess. This haplotype means grounding is critical to mitochondrial function and proper nuclear expression. People who are loosely coupled are set up to have higher levels of mitochondrial heteroplasmy rates by design. Why? As heteroplasmy rate increases when one is ground in a low solar environment this allows mitochondria to uncouple ox/phos and burn fats to make heat from the mitochondria to shrink the respiratory proteins that are surrounded by water. Water shrinks when it is heated and expands when it cold. As heteroplasmy rates increase due to fake light or when someone is disconnected from Earth (rubber shoes or astronauts) disease phenotypes morph dramatically because of a loss of mitochondria energy flow via electron chain transport. But what if your mitochondria functionally are bad because of your poor light and grounding choices? = high % heteroplasmy rate in tissues = poor energy flow in those tissues = organ failure? When tissue energy drops in that case you must first optimize the things a mitochondria uses to recycle via mitophagy. Heteroplasmy implies altered O2 levels called pseudohypoxia and UV light assimilation via our surfaces to maintain NAD+ levels at cytochrome 1 NADH couple. What does that mean if you have a high percentage of heteroplasmy in your mitochondria? = real bad mito-nuclear coaptation = poor redox. Food cannot fix this problem alone. What does it mean for good mito-nuclear coaptation? It means you burn fat well and you need very little food to sustain yourself because you assimilate photons and electrons from the sun and magnetic field by grounding well to activate autophagy and apoptosis to improve energy flow in organs. This is the food guru failure. ### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-07T03:07:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/34-yr-old-composer-cancer-survivor-with-heart-disease.18830/post-204891>

High carb foods only grow in high UV environments so electrons from carbohydrates should carry higher photonic power. So how does this translate to solution based biochemistry perspective? High carbohydrate diets provide more energy by fermentation because of these highly powered electrons, without causing humans actually needing to use their badly functioning mitochondria. Bacteria tend to like this situation. Where are bacteria in your body? MITOCHONDRIA ARE BACTERIA. Every surface where you assimilate light bacteria are also present. How to do bacteria and your mitonuclear coapatation work together? **RADIATION:** Until you ALTER your thinking, you will always recycle your experiences and old ideas. The beliefs of modern man are the batteries that make them fuel for the matrix in which they live. You have to begin to question the heavy metal and radiation issues when you read the science. All seafood has the antidote to mercury in it: selenium. Radiation effect on cells has been condemned by the entire scientific community as being a “dangerous thing”. This should surprising to inquiring mind, however, since there is strong scientific evidence for the molecular mechanisms of how transient “radiation drinks” could promote health. I tell my patients seafood radiation has many surprising benefits. Today we know that low doses of radiation stimulate DNA repair through the activation of four transcription factors, PARP-1, PARP2, ATM and Ku70. In response to low dose, sublethal radiation (or xenohormetic compounds). PARP-1 and PARP-2 activate two DNA repair pathways called “Single strand break repair” (SSBR) and one called “Base Excision Repair” (BER). Both of these repair single-stranded DNA breaks. In response to low dose, sublethal radiation (or xenohormetic compounds), Ku-70 activates the DNA repair pathway called “Non-homologous end joining” (NHEJ). [https://scholar.google.com/scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar)...

Science evolves even if your thinking about the subject remains stagnant. We must expand our grip of science…………or risk falling from the cliff. Think testicle cancer: and seafood.

600 million years ago the Cambrian explosion occured. What happened 600 million years ago was biology's Big bang and technology revolution. Several things changed in the environment. UV light spiked and made the surface skin of the planet's oceans more transparent to sunlight. This had a massive impact on the planet's life forms in the sea and eventually the land. Algae using photosynthesis began to make DHA and oxygen and this allowed the explosion of the marine food chain of seafood. All seafood is high in selenium. Selenium is very reactive with respect to oxygen in water and the air. At the time solar power was raised some other things occured on the surface of Earth. More UV light meant more oxygen would be liberated in the atmosphere from ozone and in the seas photosynthesis was super charged to make both DHA and oxygen at the same time in the seas. This convergence of quantum activity lead to life's Big Bang. It also lead to how protein incorporated selenium into our proteins. Oxygenation of Earth has occured in step wise fashion over 2.5 billion years. It was not until 600 million years ago that we got to 21% in the air that enable life to use oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor in mitochondria. The reason selenocysteine is handled so differently during eukaryotic protein synthesis can be traced back to the first Great Oxygenation Event covered in Nick Lane's book Oxygen. This was the period about 2.3 billion years ago when free oxygen in Earth's atmosphere suddenly spiked, due to the evolutionary emergence of plants and photosynthesis as a way to derive energy from the sun. Organisms needed to evolve ways to prevent cellular damage caused by oxidation, and selenium, a powerful antioxidant, would have been available in sea water but not used well because of the sun's spectral power. That power increased at 600 million years really putting a bottleneck on selenium incorporation into proteins. 2.3 billion years ago there likely was an already-existing processes for incorporating trace elements into proteins under a sunwithout prominet UV frequencies. As the marine food chain increased and these animals lived in deeper water they were protected from many of the light rays causing photo-oxidation. I believe the unusual mechanisms for selenium incorpoartion where due to where it had to happen..........in sea water and not air. In sea water the optics are different and with selenium being an insulator and not a conductor the normal mechansims for other minerla likely would not have worked for selenium, which is extremely reactive to light.
